# Key Decisions

The big technical and product calls I made on this project, with the reasoning. Future me reads this when I'm wondering "wait, why did I do it that way."

## 1. Static site, no framework

**Decision:** Plain HTML / CSS / vanilla JS, generated by a custom Node build script. No React, no Next, no Astro.

**Why:**
- 7 marketing pages with mostly static content
- Zero authenticated user state
- No client-side rendering needed for anything
- The org has no engineering team to maintain a framework upgrade cycle
- Lighthouse performance is essentially free when there's nothing to hydrate

**What I gave up:**
- Some developer ergonomics (no JSX, no hot reload, no component library)
- A small amount of code reuse (had to hand-roll a footer partial system)

**Would I do it again:** Yes. The site loads in under a second. The total client-side JS is one tiny `main.js` file. Editors update content via the CMS without ever touching code. The framework would have added complexity that solved problems we don't have.

## 2. Sanity over (Strapi / Contentful / Payload / Directus)

**Decision:** Use Sanity as the CMS.

**Why:**
- Free tier handles a small nonprofit comfortably (10K docs, 100K API requests/month, multi-user editing)
- Hosted Studio means editors can log in from anywhere with no infra setup
- Built-in image CDN with on-the-fly resizing and hotspot cropping
- Schemas are defined as plain JavaScript/TypeScript, version-controlled in the repo
- Strong document history and revert UX
- Active plugin ecosystem (`sanity-plugin-media` is what made it usable for a non-technical board)

**What I considered and rejected:**
- **Strapi:** great DX but you have to host it yourself. No way the org can maintain a Postgres database and a Node service forever.
- **Contentful:** free tier is too tight for this use case. Would have hit the limits within a month.
- **Payload:** love the philosophy, but same self-hosting problem as Strapi.
- **Directus:** same.
- **Hygraph (formerly GraphCMS):** decent free tier, but the editing UX is rougher than Sanity for non-technical users.
- **WordPress:** would have worked, but the editor experience is bloated, the theme system is opinionated, and the org didn't want another platform to manage.

**Would I do it again:** Yes, instantly. Sanity is the right answer for nonprofit-scale headless CMS work in 2026.

## 3. GiveLively over (Donorbox / Network for Good / Stripe direct)

**Decision:** Embed GiveLively's Simple Donation Widget on the donate page.

**Why:**
- Free for verified 501(c)(3) nonprofits, no transaction fees taken by the platform
- Stripe under the hood, so card processing is real and reliable
- Apple Pay, Google Pay, and Venmo work out of the box
- Tax receipts go out automatically with whatever post-donation message the org configures
- Embed widget is a copy-paste snippet, so we can swap it from the CMS without touching code
- Donors stay on our domain (no redirect to a third-party page)

**What I considered and rejected:**
- **Donorbox:** good UI but charges 1.5%+ in addition to Stripe fees. The org is small enough that 1.5% matters.
- **Network for Good:** $50/month minimum. Not worth it at this org's volume.
- **Stripe direct:** tempting but means handling PCI scope, tax receipt logic, recurring donations, refund flows, all of it. Too much code for a project I'm donating.
- **PayPal Giving Fund:** lower trust signal, harder to embed.

**The Branded vs Simple widget call:** GiveLively offers two widgets. The Branded one shows your campaign hero, social share buttons, and a "Start a Fundraiser" CTA inline. We tried it first and it looked terrible because it duplicated all the marketing the donate page already had. Switched to the Simple widget which is just the form, donor never leaves the page, much cleaner. Documented in the migration story.

## 4. The form backend (Vercel function + Sanity write API)

**Decision:** Newsletter signups POST to a Vercel serverless function which uses the Sanity client to create a `subscriber` document.

**Why:**
- The whole site is otherwise static, so a database service would be overkill
- Sanity already handles the storage and editor UI for everything else, no point introducing a second source of truth
- A serverless function is the smallest possible glue layer between the form and the CMS
- Editors get a familiar inbox view of submissions in the same Studio they already use
- Free on Vercel's hobby plan (100K invocations/month, way more than we need)

**What I considered and rejected:**
- **Formspree / Netlify Forms / Web3Forms:** these work, but submissions live in their dashboard, not in the org's Sanity. Splits the source of truth.
- **Mailchimp embed form:** the most "obvious" answer, but requires a Mailchimp account, an audience, and ties the form to whatever email tool the org picks. The org hasn't picked one yet.
- **Plain mailto link:** too low-tech, and you lose the ability to bulk-export later.

**The Sanity-as-form-backend pattern:** Sanity isn't designed for this, but it works perfectly. The serverless function uses a write-scoped token, validates the email, checks for duplicates with a GROQ query, and creates the doc. Editors see new submissions in the Studio sidebar within seconds. CSV export is built into the Studio for when the org eventually moves to a proper newsletter tool.

## 5. Footer as a single partial

**Decision:** Extract the footer into `templates/_footer.html` and inject it as `{{footer_html}}` in every page template.

**Why:**
- The footer was originally copy-pasted across 7 templates, ~60 lines each, ~420 lines of dead-weight duplication
- Editing the contact email or a social URL meant 7 file edits, which is the kind of thing that gets skipped or done inconsistently
- The template engine already supports nested key substitution, so injecting a partial as a content key just works

**Implementation detail:** the substitution loop in the template engine runs until stable, which means the footer's own placeholders (`{{contact_email}}`, `{{trust_pill_1}}`, etc.) get resolved in subsequent passes. No special partial syntax needed, just clever ordering.

## 6. Site Settings as a singleton with field groups

**Decision:** Every "global" value (logo, favicon, contact email, social URLs, GlobalGiving URL, address, footer description, trust pills) lives in one Sanity singleton called `siteSettings`, organized into tabs.

**Why:**
- Editors should change these in one place
- Some of these (favicon, contact email) used to be hardcoded in 7 templates
- Field groups give editors a clean tabbed interface so they're not staring at 14 fields in a single column
- One bug-fix-once-update-everywhere story instead of a hunt-and-replace

## 7. Auto-flowing grids everywhere

**Decision:** Every grid that an editor can add items to uses `grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(Npx, 1fr))`. No fixed column counts.

**Why:**
- The team grid, the partners grid, the staff grid, the donation tier grid all need to handle "however many items the editor decides to add"
- Hardcoded `repeat(4, 1fr)` breaks the layout when the editor adds a 5th or removes one
- Auto-fill grids never break, no matter how many items are in them
- Lower CSS surface area, no media queries needed

## 8. Drafts vs Published toggle (a Sanity gotcha worth documenting)

**Decision:** Document the perspective toggle prominently in the editor training guide.

**Why:**
- Sanity opens documents in Published view by default, which is read-only
- New editors hit a field and wonder why typing doesn't work
- This is the #1 source of "the website is broken" support emails for new Sanity installations
- Half a screen in the training doc saves 10 emails

## 9. The training doc as part of the site

**Decision:** `training.html` is built and served at `/training` on the live site, not buried in a Notion doc or PDF.

**Why:**
- Editors can bookmark a real URL
- Updates are auto-deployed alongside the rest of the site
- The doc lives next to the code that it documents, so changes stay in sync
- It's printable and mobile-friendly
- It uses the org's brand styling so it feels like part of the project, not an afterthought

## 10. The migration script as a one-shot, not a sync

**Decision:** `scripts/migrate-to-sanity.js` is a one-time importer, not a live sync.

**Why:**
- The data only needed to move once, from the old static fallback (and the legacy Google Sheets era) into Sanity
- A live sync would have required reconciliation logic, conflict resolution, idempotency keys, all the things that bidirectional sync needs
- One-shot import is ~150 lines. Live sync would be 1000+
- After the migration, Sanity is the single source of truth

## 11. Use deterministic Sanity document IDs in the migration

**Decision:** The migration script uses `_id` values like `teamMember-1`, `classroom-15`, `siteSettings`, etc. instead of letting Sanity auto-generate UUIDs.

**Why:**
- Singletons need a fixed `_id` so the studio's "edit this one document" affordance can find it
- Collections with fixed IDs make the migration script idempotent (re-running doesn't create duplicates)
- Hyphens in IDs, not periods. Periods turn out to be a Sanity gotcha (they're treated as system documents with restricted access). I learned that the hard way.

## 12. Removed `recentDonation` and `donationAmount` from Sanity entirely

**Decision:** When the donate page was rewritten around the GiveLively widget, the old "Recent Donations" feed and the custom amount tier buttons became orphans. I deleted both schemas, not just the templates that referenced them.

**Why:**
- Dead schemas in the studio sidebar confuse editors ("what is this for? should I edit it?")
- Dead code paths in the build script obscure the real data flow
- If we ever need them back, they're a few lines of YAML away

## 13. Tone down the "you can't break it" messaging in the training doc

**Decision:** Replaced the original "you can't break it, go ahead and experiment" callout with a more measured "most edits are reversible, but Delete and Unpublish are not" warning.

**Why:**
- The first version invited recklessness, especially around Delete
- Sanity's history feature reverts text and image edits but doesn't recover deleted documents easily
- A non-technical editor reading "you can't break it" will absolutely click Delete on the homepage and be very confused

The current text walks the line: reassuring about typos and small accidents, deliberate about destructive actions. Editor confidence is good, editor recklessness is bad.
